analysis.brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for risk of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test with a vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day. The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale to account for the potential time-varying differences in vaccine protection between the two brands.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test; covid-19 related A&E attendance; and unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission. Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included, without differentiation between symptomatic and asymptomatic infection.” Predictor: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls: “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” “PLR models are computationally expensive to fit … a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected … and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example reporting: “By six weeks (42 days) post-vaccination … the ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 absolute risk difference per 1000 people … for a positive SARS-CoV-2 test was -0.24 (95% confidence interval -1.71 to 1.22) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

comparisons.dimension_definition: The specific type of statistical test used for this analysis (e.g., logistic regression, Hazard Ratio).

result.paper_value: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day. The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale to account for the potential time-varying differences in vaccine protection between the two brands.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering. Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …” [...] “PLR models are computationally expensive to fit … a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.” [...] “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
result.explanation: The code implements daily-risk PLR with a vaccine-specific spline whose knots are based on event times, uses clustered sandwich SEs, delta-method CIs for marginal curves, and samples 50,000 event-free with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter specifications. Region-specific calendar-time adjustment is included via a spline-by-region interaction as described.

Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator … Confidence intervals … were obtained using the delta method …” [...] “... adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …” [...] “... a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” [...] “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”

result.code_value:
survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1),
        survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse) + pmin(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse), [...] diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se,
      diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se,
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals and reports 95% CIs. The code computes SEs for cumulative measures and constructs 95% CIs using qnorm(0.025/0.975), matching the paper’s inference approach.

result.code_value:
vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1[...]formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)[...]nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
}[...]formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region
result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination with knots based on event-time quartiles and a spline-by-region interaction. The code codes vaccine as 0/1 (vax1_az), interacts it with a spline built from event-time quantiles via nsevents(), and includes ns(vax1_day, 3)*region.

analysis.brief_description: Pooled logistic regression for positive SARS-CoV-2 test comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 with vaccine-specific time spline, adjusted for calendar time by region and additional demographic characteristics.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day. The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test … Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included …” Predictor: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls: “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …” Demographic covariates (for confounder adjustment): “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region … rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village) … All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes the calendar-time/region terms from model 1: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.”
analysis.parameter_specification: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used … a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting in Results: “By six weeks (42 days) … absolute risk difference … was -0.24 (95% confidence interval -1.71 to 1.22) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

evidence.paper_span: Methods – Outcomes and Statistical analysis: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test; covid-19 related A&E attendance; and unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission. Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included, without differentiation between symptomatic and asymptomatic infection.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region (East of England, Midlands, London, North East and Yorkshire, North West, South East, South West), number of conditions in the clinically "at risk" (but not clinically extremely vulnerable) classification, as per national prioritisation guidelines, the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village), evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (positive test or covid-19 related hospitalisation), learning disabilities, and severe mental illness.”
evidence.code_lines: model_plr.R ~120-220, 250-320; design.R ~40-75 (metadata_outcomes), ~80-110 (formula_demog, formula_comorbs)

analysis.brief_description: Pooled logistic regression for positive SARS-CoV-2 test comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 with vaccine-specific time spline, adjusted for calendar time by region and additional demographic characteristics.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day. The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test … Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included …” Predictor: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls: “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …” Demographic covariates (for confounder adjustment): “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region … rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village) … All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes the calendar-time/region terms from model 1: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.”
analysis.parameter_specification: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used … a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting in Results: “By six weeks (42 days) … absolute risk difference … was -0.24 (95% confidence interval -1.71 to 1.22) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering. Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.explanation: The code implements the paper’s parameters: event-time quartile knots for a vaccine-specific restricted cubic spline, region-specific calendar-time spline interacting with region, clustered sandwich SEs, delta-method variances for marginal curves, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weighting.

Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering. Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”

result.code_value:
# calculates robust CIs taking into account patient-level clustering [...] vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0") [...] # calculate variance of adjusted survival / adjusted cumulative incidence [...] variance <- rowSums(crossprod(t(partial_derivative), vcov) * partial_derivative) # t x 1 [...] survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1),
        survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse) + pmin(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) [...] diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se,
      diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se,
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method CIs for marginal cumulative incidence with clustered sandwich SEs; the code computes cluster-robust vcov (vcovCL) and derives CIs via partial-derivative variance (delta method) with normal quantiles.

Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).”

result.paper_value: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” [...] “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.”
result.code_value:
vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)
result.explanation: The paper describes a vaccine-specific spline for time since vaccination with knots at event-time quartiles, and the code implements this via nsevents with AZ coded as 1 and its interaction with the spline. This matches the stated coding approach.

analysis.brief_description: Fully adjusted pooled logistic regression main analysis for positive SARS-CoV-2 test comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2, adjusted for calendar time/region, demographics, and clinical characteristics; used to derive time-varying hazard ratios and marginal cumulative incidence and absolute risk differences.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …” and “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence for chadOx1 and bnt162b2, their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test … Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records … Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included …” Predictor and time-varying effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” Covariates available for adjustment: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality … evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes model 1 and 2 components: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region … additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected … and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence … their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting: “At 20 weeks (140 days) post-vaccination … the absolute risk difference per 1000 people for a positive SARS-CoV-2 test was 2.04 (0.04 to 4.04) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis; Results: comparative effectiveness, Fig 2)

result.code_value: "metadata_outcomes <- tribble(  ~outcome, ~outcome_var, ~outcome_descr,  "test", "covid_test_date", "SARS-CoV-2 test",  "postest", "positive_test_date", "Positive SARS-CoV-2 test", ... )" [...] "if(timescale==\"timesincevax\"){  formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)  formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region  formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) }" [...] "formula_demog <- . ~ . + poly(age, degree=2, raw=TRUE) + sex + imd_Q5 + ethnicity_combined + rural_urban_group" [...] "formula_comorbs <- . ~ . +  prior_covid_infection +  prior_tests_cat +  multimorb +  learndis +  sev_mental" [...] "formula3_ns <- formula_vaxonly_ns %>% update(formula_spacetime) %>% update(formula_demog) %>% update (formula_comorbs) %>% update(formula_exclude_prior_infection)"
result.explanation: The code models the same outcome and exposure with a time-since-vaccination spline and adjusts for region-specific calendar time, demographics, and clinical covariates as described. Variable roles (outcome, predictor, and controls) align with the paper’s fully adjusted PLR specification.

analysis.brief_description: Fully adjusted pooled logistic regression main analysis for positive SARS-CoV-2 test comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2, adjusted for calendar time/region, demographics, and clinical characteristics; used to derive time-varying hazard ratios and marginal cumulative incidence and absolute risk differences.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …” and “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence for chadOx1 and bnt162b2, their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test … Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records … Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included …” Predictor and time-varying effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” Covariates available for adjustment: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality … evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes model 1 and 2 components: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region … additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected … and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence … their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting: “At 20 weeks (140 days) post-vaccination … the absolute risk difference per 1000 people for a positive SARS-CoV-2 test was 2.04 (0.04 to 4.04) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis; Results: comparative effectiveness, Fig 2)

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.code_value:
formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] robustSEs <- lmtest::coeftest(model, vcov. = sandwich::vcovCL(model, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0")) [...] vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0") [...] sample_outcome = sample_nonoutcomes_n(!is.na(tte_outcome), patient_id, samplesize_nonoutcomes_n),

    sample_weights = sample_weights(!is.na(tte_outcome), sample_outcome) [...] parglm(
  formula = formula3_ns,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
) [...] action_model("postest", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000) [...] cmlinc_variance <- function(model, vcov, newdata, id, time, weights){
  # calculate variance of adjusted survival / adjusted cumulative incidence
  [...]
  variance <- rowSums(crossprod(t(partial_derivative), vcov) * partial_derivative)
} [...] diff.se = sqrt(c(
        c(0, riskdiff_variance(plrmod0, vcov0, mutate(data_plr, vax1_az=0L), mutate(data_plr, vax1_az=1L), data_plr$patient_id, data_plr$tstop, data_plr$sample_weights)),
        [...]
      )) [...] survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse)
        survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse)
result.explanation: The code sets knots at event-time quartiles for the time-varying spline, uses clustered sandwich standard errors, applies delta-method variance for marginal cumulative incidence (and differences), and implements sampling of 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameters.

vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)
result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific three-knot spline based on event-time quartiles; the code encodes AZ as 1 (Pfizer as 0) and implements an interaction with a spline whose knots are set by event-time quantiles. This matches the stated coding approach.

analysis.brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for covid-19–related A&E attendance with vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … covid-19 related A&E attendance … Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 (‘covid-19, virus identified’) or U07.2 (‘covid-19, virus not identified’) ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” Predictor and time-varying effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (calendar-time/region only): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used … a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting across outcomes: “By six weeks (42 days) … for covid-19 related A&E attendances was 0.01 (-0.27 to 0.28) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

analysis.brief_description: Calendar-time/region–adjusted pooled logistic regression comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 for covid-19–related A&E attendance with vaccine-specific time-varying effect.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … covid-19 related A&E attendance … Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 (‘covid-19, virus identified’) or U07.2 (‘covid-19, virus not identified’) ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” Predictor and time-varying effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (calendar-time/region only): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy was used … a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting across outcomes: “By six weeks (42 days) … for covid-19 related A&E attendances was 0.01 (-0.27 to 0.28) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.” [...] “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
result.explanation: The code implements event-time-based spline knots (df=4 giving three internal knots), region-by-calendar-time adjustment, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method variances for marginal curves, and sampling of 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter settings. It also uses a 20-week horizon without censoring at second dose to reflect the intention-to-treat specification.

Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.” [...] “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”

result.paper_value: Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).
result.code_value: mean.survivalse0 = sqrt(cmlinc_variance(plrmod0, vcov0, ccf, ccf$patient_id, ccf$tstop, ccf$sample_weights)) [...] survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1), [...] survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse) + pmin(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse), [...] diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se, [...] diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se,
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals for marginal cumulative incidence and their difference. The code computes variances via cmlinc_variance/riskdiff_variance and uses qnorm(0.025/0.975) to form 95% CIs, implementing the delta method.

result.code_value:
vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region
result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific spline for time since vaccination and a spline for vaccination date interacted with region. The code implements this by coding vaccine type as a 0/1 indicator (vax1_az), interacting it with an event-time–based spline of time since vaccination, and including ns(vax1_day, 3)*region.

result.paper_value: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test; covid-19 related A&E attendance; and unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission.” [...] “Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 ("covid-19, virus identified") or U07.2 ("covid-19, virus not identified") ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” [...] “adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region (East of England, Midlands, London, North East and Yorkshire, North West, South East, South West), number of conditions in the clinically "at risk" (but not clinically extremely vulnerable) classification, as per national prioritisation guidelines, the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village), evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (positive test or covid-19 related hospitalisation), learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.”
result.code_value:
"metadata_outcomes <- tribble(
  ~outcome, ~outcome_var, ~outcome_descr,
  "test", "covid_test_date", "SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "postest", "positive_test_date", "Positive SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "emergency", "emergency_date", "A&E attendance",
  "covidemergency", "emergency_covid_date", "COVID-19 A&E attendance",
  "admitted", "admitted_date", "Unplanned hospitalisation",
  "covidadmitted", "covidadmitted_date", "COVID-19 hospitalisation",
  "covidcc", "covidcc_date", "COVID-19 critical care",
  "coviddeath", "coviddeath_date", "COVID-19 death",
  "noncoviddeath", "noncoviddeath_date", "Non-COVID-19 death",
  "death", "death_date", "Any death",
  "seconddose", "covid_vax_any_2_date", "Second dose",
)" [...] "vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1" [...] "if(timescale=="timesincevax"){
  formula_timescale_pw <- . ~ . + timesincevax_pw # spline for timescale only
  formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only
  formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments

  formula_timesincevax_pw <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:timesincevax_pw
  formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)

}" [...] "formula_demog <- . ~ . + poly(age, degree=2, raw=TRUE) + sex + imd_Q5 + ethnicity_combined + rural_urban_group" [...] "formula_comorbs <- . ~ . +
  prior_covid_infection +
  prior_tests_cat +
  multimorb +
  learndis +
  sev_mental" [...] "formula3_ns <- formula_vaxonly_ns %>% update(formula_spacetime) %>% update(formula_demog) %>% update (formula_comorbs) %>% update(formula_exclude_prior_infection)"
result.explanation: The paper specifies the outcome (COVID-19 A&E attendance), exposure (vaccine type with a vaccine-specific time spline), and controls (region–calendar time interaction, demographics, and clinical factors). The code implements the same: maps the ‘covidemergency’ outcome, uses vax1_az with a time-since-vaccination spline and region-by-date spline, and includes demographic and clinical covariates.

analysis.brief_description: Pooled logistic regression for covid-19–related A&E attendance comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 with vaccine-specific time spline, adjusted for calendar time by region and additional demographic characteristics.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … covid-19 related A&E attendance … Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 … or U07.2 … ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” Predictor and time-varying effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls: “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …” Demographic covariates available: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) … ethnicity … NHS region … rurality … All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Plus calendar-time/region terms: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … were obtained using the delta method …” “a sampling strategy … a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example reporting: “By six weeks (42 days) … for covid-19 related A&E attendances was 0.01 (-0.27 to 0.28) …”

vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region [...] probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific spline for time since vaccination and a region-by-calendar-time spline; the code codes vaccine as a 0/1 indicator and implements vax–time spline interactions and ns(vax1_day, 3)*region with event-time–based knots. This matches the described coding approach.

analysis.brief_description: Fully adjusted pooled logistic regression main analysis for covid-19–related A&E attendance comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2; used to produce marginal cumulative incidence, absolute risk differences, and hazard ratios over time.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …” and “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence for chadOx1 and bnt162b2, their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … covid-19 related A&E attendance … Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 … or U07.2 … ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” Predictor/time effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” Covariates available for adjustment: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … IMD … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests … rurality … evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes the calendar-time/region and demographic adjustments from models 1 and 2.
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence … their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting: “At 20 weeks (140 days) … for covid-19 A&E attendances was 0.06 (-0.31 to 0.43) …”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis; Results: comparative effectiveness, Fig 2)

"emergency_covid_date = pmin(emergency_covid_date, covidadmitted_date, na.rm=TRUE)," [...] "metadata_outcomes <- tribble( ~outcome, ~outcome_var, ~outcome_descr, [...] "covidemergency", "emergency_covid_date", "COVID-19 A&E attendance", [...] )" [...] "if(timescale=="timesincevax"){ [...] formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) }" [...] "formula_demog <- . ~ . + poly(age, degree=2, raw=TRUE) + sex + imd_Q5 + ethnicity_combined + rural_urban_group" [...] "formula_comorbs <- . ~ . +
  prior_covid_infection +
  prior_tests_cat +
  multimorb +
  learndis +

analysis.brief_description: Fully adjusted pooled logistic regression main analysis for covid-19–related A&E attendance comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2; used to produce marginal cumulative incidence, absolute risk differences, and hazard ratios over time.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …” and “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence for chadOx1 and bnt162b2, their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … covid-19 related A&E attendance … Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 … or U07.2 … ICD-10 diagnosis codes.” Predictor/time effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” Covariates available for adjustment: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … IMD … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests … rurality … evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence … their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting: “At 20 weeks (140 days) … for covid-19 A&E attendances was 0.06 (-0.31 to 0.43) …”

vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] timesincevax_pw = droplevels(timesince_cut(tstop, postvaxcuts)) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region
result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific restricted cubic spline over time since vaccination and a region-specific calendar-time spline; the code codes vaccine as a 0/1 indicator (vax1_az), uses an event-time-based spline interacted with vax1_az, and includes ns(vax1_day, 3)*region. These coding choices align.

Unplanned covid-19 related hospital admissions were identified using HES inpatient hospital records with U07.1 or U07.2 reason for admission ICD-10 codes.”[...]“The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.”[...]“Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.”[...]“Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region (East of England, Midlands, London, North East and Yorkshire, North West, South East, South West), number of conditions in the clinically "at risk" (but not clinically extremely vulnerable) classification, as per national prioritisation guidelines, the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village), evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (positive test or covid-19 related hospitalisation), learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.”

analysis.brief_description: Pooled logistic regression for unplanned covid-19–related hospital admission comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 with vaccine-specific time spline, adjusted for calendar time by region and additional demographic characteristics.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission. Unplanned covid-19 related hospital admissions were identified using HES inpatient hospital records with U07.1 or U07.2 reason for admission ICD-10 codes.” Predictor/time effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls: “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics …” Demographic covariates available: “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … IMD … ethnicity … NHS region … rurality … All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Plus calendar-time/region terms: “adjusting for region-specific calendar-time effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region.”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting across outcomes: “By six weeks (42 days) … for covid-19 related hospital admissions was 0.03 (-0.22 to 0.27).”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis)

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “PLR models are computationally expensive to fit, as the input dataset must be arranged as one row per person per day of follow-up. To manage this, a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.code_value:
“nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
}” [...] “if(timescale=="timesincevax"){ [...] formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only [...] formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) }” [...] “robustSEs <- lmtest::coeftest(model, vcov. = sandwich::vcovCL(model, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0"))” [...] “vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0")” [...] “cmlinc_variance <- function(model, vcov, newdata, id, time, weights){

  # calculate variance of adjusted survival / adjusted cumulative incidence” [...] “# partial derivative of cumulative incidence at t” [...] “variance <- rowSums(crossprod(t(partial_derivative), vcov) * partial_derivative) # t x 1” [...] “sample_outcome = sample_nonoutcomes_n(!is.na(tte_outcome), patient_id, samplesize_nonoutcomes_n),

    sample_weights = sample_weights(!is.na(tte_outcome), sample_outcome),” [...] “plrmod3 <- parglm(
  formula = formula3_pw,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
)” [...] “parglmparams <- parglm.control(
  method = "LINPACK",
  nthreads = 8,
  maxit = 40 # default = 25
)” [...] “action_model("covidadmitted", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000),”
result.explanation: The code implements event-time-based natural spline knots with df=4 (yielding quartiles), clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method style variance for marginal cumulative incidence, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter specifications. It also adjusts calendar time by region via a spline interaction as described.

evidence.paper_span: Methods: statistical analysis — “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “To manage this, a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”

vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 […] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen

  probs <- seq(0,df)/df

This matches the described coding approach.

analysis.brief_description: Fully adjusted pooled logistic regression main analysis for unplanned covid-19–related hospital admission comparing ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2; used to derive marginal cumulative incidence, absolute risk differences, and hazard ratios over time.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …” and “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence for chadOx1 and bnt162b2, their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission. Unplanned covid-19 related hospital admissions were identified using HES inpatient hospital records with U07.1 or U07.2 reason for admission ICD-10 codes.” Predictor/time effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” Covariates available for adjustment: “Participant characteristics … for confounder adjustment include age, sex … IMD … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests … rurality … evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness. All characteristics were ascertained as at the time of vaccination.” Also includes the calendar-time/region and demographic adjustments from models 1 and 2.
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “For each outcome based on the fully adjusted model, the marginal cumulative incidence … their difference, and the hazard ratio are shown.” “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and example CI reporting: “At 20 weeks (140 days) … for covid-19 hospital admissions was 0.11 (-0.22 to 0.44).”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Statistical analysis; Results: comparative effectiveness, Fig 2)

comparisons.dimension_definition: The specific type of statistical test used for this analysis (e.g., logistic regression, Hazard Ratio).

result.paper_value: We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) 6 7 with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day.
result.code_value: # fits some pooled logistic regression models, with different adjustment sets

"covidadmitted", "covidadmitted_date", "COVID-19 hospitalisation", [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){ [...] q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)

result.paper_value: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.explanation: The code implements a three-knot spline at event-time quartiles, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method style variance for marginal cumulative incidence, and samples 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weighting, matching the paper’s parameters.

survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1),
        survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse) + pmin(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse)[...]
      diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se,
      diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals with clustered SEs, and the code implements this by deriving variances and computing 95% CIs using qnorm(0.025/0.975). This matches the stated inference approach.

data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  ) [...] metadata_outcomes <- tribble(
  ~outcome, ~outcome_var, ~outcome_descr,
  "test", "covid_test_date", "SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "postest", "positive_test_date", "Positive SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "emergency", "emergency_date", "A&E attendance",
  "covidemergency", "emergency_covid_date", "COVID-19 A&E attendance",
  "admitted", "admitted_date", "Unplanned hospitalisation",
  "covidadmitted", "covidadmitted_date", "COVID-19 hospitalisation",
  "covidcc", "covidcc_date", "COVID-19 critical care",
  "coviddeath", "coviddeath_date", "COVID-19 death",
  "noncoviddeath", "noncoviddeath_date", "Non-COVID-19 death",
  "death", "death_date", "Any death",
  "seconddose", "covid_vax_any_2_date", "Second dose",
) [...] if(timescale=="timesincevax"){
  formula_timescale_pw <- . ~ . + timesincevax_pw # spline for timescale only
  formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only
  formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments

  formula_timesincevax_pw <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:timesincevax_pw
  formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)

} [...] if(exclude_prior_infection==1L){
  formula_exclude_prior_infection <- . ~ . - prior_covid_infection
} else(
  formula_exclude_prior_infection <- . ~ .
) [...] formula_demog <- . ~ . + poly(age, degree=2, raw=TRUE) + sex + imd_Q5 + ethnicity_combined + rural_urban_group [...] formula_comorbs <- . ~ . +
  prior_covid_infection +
  prior_tests_cat +
  multimorb +
  learndis +
  sev_mental [...] formula3_ns <- formula_vaxonly_ns %>% update(formula_spacetime) %>% update(formula_demog) %>% update (formula_comorbs) %>% update(formula_exclude_prior_infection)
result.explanation: Both describe restricting to those without prior infection, using positive SARS‑CoV‑2 test as the outcome, modeling vaccine type with a time‑since‑vaccination spline, and adjusting for region-specific calendar time, demographics, and clinical factors. The code also removes prior_covid_infection from covariates under this restriction, aligning with the subgroup definition.

Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included, without differentiation between symptomatic and asymptomatic infection.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region (East of England, Midlands, London, North East and Yorkshire, North West, South East, South West), number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ (but not clinically extremely vulnerable) classification, as per national prioritisation guidelines, the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village), evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (positive test or covid-19 related hospitalisation), learning disabilities, and severe mental illness.”

Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included, without differentiation between symptomatic and asymptomatic infection.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” [...] “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” [...] “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex (male or female), English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD, grouped by quintiles), ethnicity (Black, Mixed, South Asian, White, Other, as per the UK census), NHS region (East of England, Midlands, London, North East and Yorkshire, North West, South East, South West), number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ (but not clinically extremely vulnerable) classification, as per national prioritisation guidelines, the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests (positive or negative) in the 90 days before the study start date (via SGSS), rurality (urban conurbation, urban city or town, rural town or village), evidence of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (positive test or covid-19 related hospitalisation), learning disabilities, and severe mental illness.”",

  "test", "covid_test_date", "SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "postest", "positive_test_date", "Positive SARS-CoV-2 test",
  "emergency", "emergency_date", "A&E attendance",
  "covidemergency", "emergency_covid_date", "COVID-19 A&E attendance",
  "admitted", "admitted_date", "Unplanned hospitalisation",
  "covidadmitted", "covidadmitted_date", "COVID-19 hospitalisation",
  "covidcc", "covidcc_date", "COVID-19 critical care",
  "coviddeath", "coviddeath_date", "COVID-19 death",
  "noncoviddeath", "noncoviddeath_date", "Non-COVID-19 death",
  "death", "death_date", "Any death",
  "seconddose", "covid_vax_any_2_date", "Second dose",
) [...] if(timescale=="timesincevax"){
  formula_timescale_pw <- . ~ . + timesincevax_pw # spline for timescale only
  formula_timescale_ns <- . ~ . + nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) # spline for timescale only
  formula_spacetime <- . ~ . + ns(vax1_day, 3)*region # spline for space-time adjustments

  formula_timesincevax_pw <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:timesincevax_pw
  formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4)

} [...] if(exclude_prior_infection==1L){
  formula_exclude_prior_infection <- . ~ . - prior_covid_infection
} else(
  formula_exclude_prior_infection <- . ~ .
) [...] formula_demog <- . ~ . + poly(age, degree=2, raw=TRUE) + sex + imd_Q5 + ethnicity_combined + rural_urban_group [...] formula_comorbs <- . ~ . +
  prior_covid_infection +
  prior_tests_cat +
  multimorb +
  learndis +
  sev_mental [...] formula3_ns <- formula_vaxonly_ns %>% update(formula_spacetime) %>% update(formula_demog) %>% update (formula_comorbs) %>% update(formula_exclude_prior_infection)",
  "explanation": "Both describe restricting to those without prior infection, using positive SARS‑CoV‑2 test as the outcome, modeling vaccine type with a time‑since‑vaccination spline, and adjusting for region-specific calendar time, demographics, and clinical factors. The code also removes prior_covid_infection from covariates under this restriction, aligning with the subgroup definition.",

analysis.brief_description: Sensitivity analysis: fully adjusted pooled logistic regression restricted to participants with no prior SARS-CoV-2 infection for outcome of positive SARS-CoV-2 test.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” (Main analysis specification:) “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale … The effect is permitted to vary over the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Restriction: “repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: positive SARS-CoV-2 test … Positive SARS-CoV-2 tests were identified using SGSS records and based on swab date. Both polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and lateral flow positive tests were included …” Predictor/time effect: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” Controls (fully adjusted as in main analysis): “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.” and “Participant characteristics used to describe the cohort and for confounder adjustment include age, sex … IMD … ethnicity … NHS region … number of conditions in the clinically ‘at risk’ … the number of SARS-CoV-2 tests … rurality … learning disabilities, and severe mental illness …”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” Result summary for this sensitivity: “A sensitivity analysis restricting comparative effectiveness estimates to those with no prior evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection showed similar results (supplementary fig S5).”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Sensitivity analyses; Results: sensitivity analysis mentioned)
