result.paper_value: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.”[...]“The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.”[...]“Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.”[...]“Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.”[...]“Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).”[...]“a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.code_value:
data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  )[...]nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
}[...]vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0")[...]sample_outcome = sample_nonoutcomes_n(!is.na(tte_outcome), patient_id, samplesize_nonoutcomes_n),

    sample_weights = sample_weights(!is.na(tte_outcome), sample_outcome),[...]action_model("postest", "timesincevax", "0", "plr", 50000, "1")[...]variance <- rowSums(crossprod(t(partial_derivative), vcov) * partial_derivative)
result.explanation: The code filters to participants with no prior infection and implements the same PLR parameter choices as described: spline knots based on event-time quartiles, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta‑method variance, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights. Therefore the parameter specification for this sensitivity analysis matches the paper.

evidence.paper_span: sensitivity analyses: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.”[...] statistical analysis: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times. Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering. Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance). … a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
evidence.code_lines: model_plr.R: ~80-90 (filter prior infection), ~150-170 (nsevents with quartiles), ~270-285 (vcovCL); ~105-135 (sampling and weights). report_plr.R: ~45-60 (nsevents quartiles), ~205-260 (cmlinc_variance with rowSums(crossprod(...))), ~295-355 (riskdiff_variance with rowSums(crossprod(...))). create-project.R: ~570-620 (action_model("postest", "timesincevax", "0", "plr", 50000, "1")).

result.code_value:
survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1),
        survival.ul = pmin(1, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse) + pmin(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse),
[...]
      diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se,
      diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se,
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals for cumulative incidence and their differences, and the code implements 95% normal-approximation CIs using qnorm with standard errors derived from variance calculations consistent with the delta method. This matches the inference approach for the sensitivity analysis that repeats the main analysis in the no-prior-infection subgroup.

result.paper_value: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” [...] “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “To manage this, a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”
result.code_value:
data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  ) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] plrmod3 <- parglm(
  formula = formula3_ns,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
) [...] vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0") [...] sample_weights <- function(had_outcome, sampled){
  # `had_outcome` is a boolean indicating if the subject has experienced the outcome or not
  # `sampled` is a boolean indicating if the patient is to be sampled or not
  case_when(
    had_outcome ~ 1,
    !had_outcome & !sampled ~ 0,
    !had_outcome & sampled ~ sum(!had_outcome)/sum((sampled) & !had_outcome),
    TRUE ~ NA_real_
  )
} [...] "Confidence intervals for the risk-adjusted survival rates are calculated using the delta method (i.e, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance of the cumulative incidence)." [...] action_model("covidadmitted", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000, "1"),
result.explanation: The code implements the sensitivity restriction and uses event-time-based spline knots, clustered sandwich standard errors, delta-method CIs, and sampling 50,000 non-events with inverse-probability weights, matching the paper’s parameter settings. These parameters apply to the fully adjusted PLR used for the covidadmitted outcome.

evidence.paper_span: sensitivity analyses: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” [...] statistical analysis: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination. Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” [...] “Standard errors for the PLR model were obtained using the clustered sandwich estimator to account for within-participant clustering.” [...] “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).” [...] “a sampling strategy was used such that all those who experienced the event of interest are selected (to retain statistical power), and a random sample of 50 000 event-free participants are selected. Persontime is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability to recover the characteristics of the complete cohort.”

  "code_value": "data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  ) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])
} [...] formula_timesincevax_ns <- . ~ . + vax1_az + vax1_az:nsevents(tstop, outcome_event, 4) [...] plrmod3 <- parglm(
  formula = formula3_ns,
  data = data_plr,
  weights = sample_weights,
  family = binomial,
  control = parglmparams,
  na.action = "na.fail",
  model = FALSE
) [...] vcov <- vcovCL(plrmod, cluster = cluster, type = "HC0") [...] sample_weights <- function(had_outcome, sampled){
  # `had_outcome` is a boolean indicating if the subject has experienced the outcome or not
  # `sampled` is a boolean indicating if the patient is to be sampled or not
  case_when(
    had_outcome ~ 1,
    !had_outcome & !sampled ~ 0,
    !had_outcome & sampled ~ sum(!had_outcome)/sum((sampled) & !had_outcome),
    TRUE ~ NA_real_
  )
} [...] "Confidence intervals for the risk-adjusted survival rates are calculated using the delta method (i.e, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance of the cumulative incidence)." [...] action_model("covidadmitted", "timesincevax", "1", "plr", 50000, "1"),",

analysis.brief_description: Sensitivity analysis: fully adjusted pooled logistic regression restricted to participants with no prior SARS-CoV-2 infection for outcome of unplanned covid-19–related hospital admission.
analysis.analysis_specification: “We conducted a post-hoc sensitivity analysis that repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” (Main PLR:) “We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) … with time since vaccination as the timescale …”
analysis.variable_specification: Restriction: “repeated the main analysis in the subgroup of participants who had no evidence of a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.” Outcome: “Three outcomes were defined: … unplanned covid-19 related hospital admission. Unplanned covid-19 related hospital admissions were identified using HES inpatient hospital records with U07.1 or U07.2 reason for admission ICD-10 codes.” Predictor/time effect and controls as in fully adjusted main analysis: “The PLR model included vaccine type, and a vaccine-specific three-knot restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination.” “Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders … 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.”
analysis.parameter_specification: “Knot locations were based on quartiles of the event times.” “Standard errors … clustered sandwich estimator …” “Confidence intervals … delta method …” “a sampling strategy … 50 000 event-free participants … Person-time is weighted by the inverse of the sampling probability …” “Using an intention-to-treat approach, comparative effectiveness estimates beyond 14 weeks were considered to be second-dose effects.”
analysis.inference_specification: “Confidence intervals for the vaccine-specific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method …” and overall sensitivity result: “A sensitivity analysis restricting comparative effectiveness estimates to those with no prior evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection showed similar results (supplementary fig S5).”
analysis.location: In-text (Methods: Sensitivity analyses; Results: sensitivity analysis mentioned)

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any pre-specified inference criteria used for the analysis (e.g., alpha = 0.05, confidence intervals excluding particular values).

result.paper_value: Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).
result.code_value:
survival.ll = pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.025)*survivalse) - pmax(0, survival+qnorm(0.975)*survivalse - 1)[...]diff.ll = diff+qnorm(0.025)*diff.se,
      diff.ul = diff+qnorm(0.975)*diff.se,
result.explanation: The paper specifies delta-method confidence intervals for cumulative incidence and their differences. The code computes variances and then uses qnorm(0.025/0.975) to form 95% normal-approximation intervals for survival and risk differences, consistent with the delta method.

evidence.paper_span: “Confidence intervals for the vaccinespecific marginal cumulative incidence, and their difference, were obtained using the delta method (that is, a first-order Taylor series approximation of the variance).”

data_cohort <- read_rds(here("output", "data", "data_cohort.rds")) %>%
  filter(
    prior_covid_infection==0 | (exclude_prior_infection!=1L)
  ) [...] vax1_az = (vax1_type=="az")*1 [...] timesincevax_pw = droplevels(timesince_cut(tstop, postvaxcuts)) [...] nsevents <- function(x, events, df){
  # this is the same as the `ns` function,
  # except the knot locations are chosen
  # based on the event times only, not on all person-time
  probs <- seq(0,df)/df
  q <- quantile(x[events==1], probs=probs)
  ns(x, knots=q[-c(1, df+1)], Boundary.knots = q[c(1, df+1)])

result.explanation: The paper specifies a vaccine-specific restricted cubic spline for time since vaccination with knots at event-time quartiles and defines the hospital admission outcome, and the sensitivity analysis excludes prior infections. The code implements an AZ vs Pfizer binary indicator, uses nsevents() to set spline knots from event times and interact with vaccine, and filters to those without prior infection for the sensitivity analysis.

result.paper_value: We compared the effectiveness of a first dose of ChAdOx1 versus BNT162b2 using pooled logistic regression (PLR) 6 7 with time since vaccination as the timescale and with the outcome risk estimated each day.[...]Covid-19 related A&E attendances were identified using HES emergency care records with U07.1 ("covid-19, virus identified") or U07.2 ("covid-19, virus not identified") ICD-10 diagnosis codes.[...]Three models were fit for each outcome, with progressive adjustment for confounders 1 : adjusting for region-specific calendartime effects, by including a two-knot restricted cubic spline for the date of vaccination and its interaction with region 2 ; additionally adjusting for demographic characteristics 3 ; additionally adjusting for clinical characteristics.[...]We also use piecewise-linear timevarying hazards (at 7-day intervals in the first 28 days, and 14-day intervals thereafter) to investigate whether the estimates were robust to our choice of spline parameters.
